items.front: What is the purpose of Class C services?
items.back: They are designed to keep ATC informed of all aircraft within Class C airspace, not to exclude operations from it.

items.front: What are the main VFR services provided in Class C airspace?
items.back: Provide sequencing to the primary airport, separation and safety alerts between IFR and VFR aircraft, and mandatory traffic advisories and safety alerts between VFR aircraft.

items.front: What separation methods may be used between VFR and IFR aircraft in Class C service?
items.back: Use visual separation, 500 feet vertical separation, or target resolution, except when ISR is displayed.

items.front: What establishes two-way communications for entry into Class C airspace?
items.back: If ATC replies with the aircraft call sign, even with 'standby,' two-way communications are established and the aircraft may enter Class C airspace.

items.front: When must a VFR aircraft assigned an off-direction altitude in Class C be told to resume appropriate VFR altitudes?
items.back: When the altitude is no longer needed for separation, when the aircraft leaves the outer area, or when Class C service is terminated.

items.front: What separation exceptions apply in Class C for helicopters, powered-lift aircraft, and hot air balloons?
items.back: VFR helicopters and powered-lift need not be separated from IFR helicopters or powered-lift, and hot air balloons need not be separated from IFR aircraft, but traffic information and safety alerts still apply.

items.front: How are departures from uncontrolled airports within Class C handled?
items.back: Use the procedures published in a Letter to Airmen.

items.front: When should Class C service terminate for aircraft landing at other than the primary airport?
items.back: Terminate it far enough away to let the pilot change frequency and obtain the appropriate traffic and airport information.
